title: The Opera Fanatic: Sociology of an Obsession By Claudio E. Benzecry. University of Chicago Press. 2011. 246 pages. $29 paper
abstract: Claudio Benzecry has written a considerable book. In size, the volume is not that big. But it is very dense, with enough material for two or three books, maybe not that coherent. Such variety makes this work vivacious and stimulating. This also makes reading it more complex than expected at first from the style of the author, which is clear and direct. “Why is it that you sociologists always ask about if I go to the opera to be seen, to meet people, to see friends, to achieve a better professional status, but always fail to ask me if I go because I like it or, better, because I love it?” (p. 194). This is the last sentence of The Opera Fanatic: Sociology of an Obsession. It does not only indicate what José Luis, an interviewee, feels. It also expresses Benzecry's own leitmotif throughout his book. Indignation is the initial impetus of his writing; then, it organizes the whole argument, which combines two related requirements: to do opera justice as a specific object of passion, and to make a sociology sensitive to this “love for” opera, without reducing it to a social parade. It is perhaps as a consequence of this program that the book is further characterized by two recurrent mottos: to show that the passion for opera does not resemble any other passion1 and that Benzecry's own approach is radically different from any other in sociology. Undoubtedly, such pugnacity is a resource that gives the writing its lively tone. This stance also proves to be the author's limitation.
